The Porter and the Sailor:
Hindbad the porter was having the worst day of his week.
He was carrying a load so heavy it felt like someone had stacked a donkey on top of another donkey and then asked him to carry both. The sun was blazing. The streets of Baghdad were packed with people who kept bumping into him. His back hurt. His feet hurt. Even his elbows hurt, which he hadn't known was possible.
He stopped to rest on the steps of the grandest house on the street. Cool air drifted from inside. He could smell roasting lamb and fresh bread and something sweet he couldn't name. Music was playing — a lute, expertly played, somewhere deeper in the house.
Hindbad sat down in the dust and said several things under his breath about the unfairness of the world.
A servant appeared at the door. "My master heard you," the servant said. "He would like you to come inside."
Hindbad was sure this was going to go badly for him. He stood up anyway, because when a rich man's servant invites you inside a rich man's house, you go inside.
The room was cool and enormous and covered in carpets so fine that Hindbad felt embarrassed to walk on them. At the head of a long table sat an old man — white-haired, richly dressed, with bright eyes and the kind of face that has seen a great deal and decided to find most of it interesting.
"I am Sinbad," the old man said. "Sinbad the Sailor. Please sit down."
A servant put food in front of Hindbad before he could say anything. He ate. The food was extraordinary.
"I heard what you said on my steps," said Sinbad. "You said the world is unfair. That some men sit in fine houses while others break their backs in the street."
Hindbad looked at his hands. "I didn't know anyone could hear me."
"I can always hear that," said Sinbad. "I used to say the same thing, when I was young and poor and angry about it." He leaned back in his chair. "I was born in Baghdad. My father was a merchant who left me money when he died — enough to live on comfortably. I spent it all. Every coin. Feasts and fine clothes and all the usual ways a young man destroys a fortune."
He paused. "When the money was gone, I had nothing. I was poor and I was embarrassed and I needed to do something about it. So I went to sea."
"You chose to go to sea?" said Hindbad.
"I did. Seven times."
Hindbad looked around at the fine house, the food, the carpets, the musicians barely visible through an archway. "And this is what the sea gave you?"
"This is what the sea cost me," said Sinbad. "There is a difference." He refilled Hindbad's cup. "Stay tonight. I'll tell you about the first voyage. Then you can decide whether my fortune came easily."
Hindbad stayed.
And Sinbad began.
~ ~ ~
The First Voyage: The Island That Moved:
I was twenty-three years old when I first went to sea, said Sinbad.
I had nothing. My father's money was gone and I was ashamed of how I'd wasted it. I sold what little I had left — a few pieces of furniture, some clothes — and bought trade goods with the money. Fabric. Spices. Small things that took up little space but could be sold for more than I'd paid for them.
I found a merchant ship leaving for the islands of the east and bought passage.
The sea was not what I expected. I had grown up in the desert heat of Baghdad. The ocean was vast in a way that land is not — you could stand at the rail and look in every direction and see nothing but water and sky, and the ship was the only solid thing in all of it. I loved it immediately. That was my first mistake, because loving the sea makes you stop being careful around it.
After several weeks we came to a beautiful island. Green trees, white sand, freshwater streams running down to the shore. The captain anchored and we went ashore to rest and cook and enjoy the land for a day.
Some men swam. Some gathered fruit. I found a soft patch of grass under a shady tree and fell asleep.
The island moved.
I don't mean it shifted slightly. I mean it lurched, tilted, and began to sink. Because it was not an island. It was a whale — an ancient whale of enormous size that had been floating still for so long that soil had built up on its back and trees had grown from the soil, and it had looked exactly like an island because it had been still for so long.
Until our cooking fires warmed it. And it woke up.
Men ran screaming for the ship. The captain, to his credit, cut the anchor rope immediately and began maneuvering to pick up survivors. The whale dove slowly, taking its trees and its grass and its freshwater stream and me with it.
I grabbed a wooden washtub that was floating nearby. I held on.
The ship disappeared. The whale disappeared. I was alone in the middle of the ocean on a wooden washtub, which is perhaps the least dignified vessel ever used for an ocean crossing.
I held on for two days. Wind and current pushed me toward an island — a real one this time, solid rock and old trees that had definitely not grown on a whale's back. I dragged myself onto the beach and lay there for a while being grateful.
I found horses on that island. Beautiful horses, being tended by grooms who worked for a king named Mihrjan. They fed me and gave me dry clothes and brought me to the king. He was generous and kind, and when he heard I was a merchant from Baghdad he gave me a position overseeing the harbor — recording the names and cargoes of ships as they came and went.
I did that work for months. I was grateful for it. But every time a ship came in from Baghdad I felt something pull in my chest like a hook.
One day a ship arrived, and the merchant overseeing its cargo was dealing with goods that looked exactly like the goods I had lost when the whale surfaced. I went closer. I read the labels on the bales.
My name was on them. My goods. Packed and labeled by my own hands.
The captain of the ship came down the gangplank and I recognized him. He had thought I was dead. He had logged my goods as cargo of a drowned man and intended to sell them and distribute the profit to my heirs.
I told him I was not dead.
He stared at me for a long time. Then he laughed and embraced me and gave me everything back.
I said goodbye to King Mihrjan and sailed for home. I arrived in Baghdad with more goods than I had left with, because I had traded carefully during my months at the harbor. I sold them at a profit and bought my father's house back and furnished it well.
And then, said Sinbad, I sat in my comfortable house for exactly six months before I started thinking about the sea again.
Hindbad looked at him. "You went back?"
"Of course I went back," said Sinbad. "That is the nature of the sea. Now go home and sleep. Come back tomorrow. I'll tell you about the second voyage."
~ ~ ~
The Second Voyage: The Valley of Diamond:
Hindbad came back the next day, and Sinbad continued.
The second voyage, said Sinbad, began the same way the first one had: I had a comfortable life, I got restless, I bought trade goods and found a ship.
We stopped at a beautiful green island to rest. I found a shady spot by a stream and fell asleep reading, which I mention only to say that I am capable of making exactly the same mistake twice.
When I woke up, the ship was gone.
I want to be clear about this. The ship had simply left. Whether they forgot to count heads or assumed I had already reboarded or simply did not notice — I cannot say. But I climbed the tallest tree I could find and looked in every direction.
Water. Just water. No sail anywhere on the horizon.
In the center of the island I found a white dome — perfectly round, perfectly smooth, taller than a house and with no door or window anywhere. I walked around it several times trying to understand what I was looking at.
Then a shadow fell over me. The shadow of something very large. The sun disappeared.
I looked up.
The largest bird I have ever seen or imagined was descending from the sky. Its wingspan blocked the light completely. It was a Roc — a bird from old stories, a bird I had assumed was exaggerated for dramatic effect.
It was not exaggerated.
It landed on the dome, which was its egg. It settled itself and tucked its enormous head under one wing.
I thought very quickly. I unwound my turban — I always wear a turban with a good length of cloth — and tied myself carefully to the Roc's leg. If there was a way off this island, this was it.
At dawn the Roc rose straight up. I held on with everything I had and kept my eyes shut until the wind told me I was very high up. We flew for what felt like half a day. Then the bird descended, fast and steep, and landed.
I untied myself and looked around.
I was at the bottom of a valley surrounded by cliff walls so sheer and so high that there was no possible way to climb them. The valley floor was covered in diamonds. Not small ones. Handfuls of them, everywhere I stepped.
I also noticed the snakes. Large snakes. Enormous snakes. Snakes thick as tree trunks, coiled in the shade of the cliff walls. They were sleeping. I discovered later that they slept during the day and hunted at night.
It was currently day. That was the good news.
The bad news was that I was at the bottom of a pit of diamonds with no way out.
Then something came down from the sky — a large piece of fresh meat, thrown from the cliff top. Then another. Then several more. I understood: this was how the diamond merchants of the region worked. They threw down meat. The diamonds stuck to the fat and blood. Eagles swooped down to carry the meat back to their nests at the top of the cliffs. The merchants then collected the diamonds from the nests.
I filled my pockets with diamonds. Then I found the largest piece of meat I could and tied myself to it, as firmly as my turban would allow.
An eagle lifted me. Very fast. Very high. At the top of the cliff, the eagle landed in its nest and a merchant shouted and waved his arms at it to drive it away from the meat — which was when he found me, tied to his dinner, covered in diamonds, somewhat shaken.
He stared. I untied myself.
"The strangest thing," he said finally, "that has ever come up from that valley."
I gave him a share of the diamonds. He gave me passage to the nearest city. I traded the remaining diamonds for an enormous profit and found a ship home to Baghdad.
Sinbad smiled at Hindbad across the table. "That is the second voyage."
"You tied yourself to a piece of meat," said Hindbad.
"I did."
"And rode an eagle out of a pit."
"I did."
"And you went to sea a third time."
Sinbad laughed. "Oh, the third time was far worse. Come back tomorrow."
~ ~ ~
The Third Voyage: The Giant's Island:
The third voyage, said Sinbad the next evening, taught me something about the difference between looking dangerous and being dangerous.
We were a week from port when we saw the island. Low and green, with a high rocky hill at the center. We went ashore for water.
From the hill, something watched us. Something large.
It came down the slope toward us with the slow confidence of an animal that has never needed to hurry because nothing has ever been a serious threat to it. It was shaped like a man but the size of a house — black skin, one eye in the center of its forehead, teeth like stone pillars, fingernails like iron hooks. It walked through the shallow water to our ship as if wading through a stream.
It picked up our captain and looked at him the way you look at a fruit when you're deciding whether to eat it.
It put him down.
It walked around the ship slowly, studying us all. Then it walked back up to its cave and went to sleep.
We held a council on the ship. The sensible thing was to sail away immediately. We had plenty of water. We could simply leave.
Nobody suggested this quickly enough. By morning the giant was back. This time it chose. It picked up our largest sailor — the strongest, fattest man among us — and ate him for breakfast, right there on the beach in front of everyone.
Then it went back to its cave.
I will not describe the next few evenings. We could not sail because the wind was against us. Each morning the giant came and ate one man. We could not sleep. We could barely eat.
On the third night, when the giant was sleeping, we built two large rafts from timber we cut from the ship. We carried sharpened stakes to the cave. We heated the stakes in the fire until the tips glowed red.
Ten of us drove the stakes into the giant's single eye at the same moment.
The scream was unlike any sound I have heard before or since. We ran for the rafts. The giant stumbled out of the cave, blind and roaring, grabbing at anything in reach. We pushed off from shore.
He couldn't see us. But he could hear us.
He picked up boulders and threw them by sound. He was extraordinarily accurate. Two of our rafts — and most of our men — were crushed. My raft and one other survived.
We drifted for two days. We landed on another island. We were exhausted and half-dead with thirst and grief for our companions.
That night, a serpent as thick as a house beam came from the trees. It swallowed one of my two remaining companions whole. We could hear him inside it.
On the second night it came back. It swallowed the other man.
I spent the third night in the highest tree I could find, tied to the branches with my turban, too frightened to sleep. At dawn I came down and built a raft and pushed off before the serpent woke.
I was very tired by the time I reached Baghdad.
Sinbad was quiet for a moment. "Eleven men sailed with me on the third voyage," he said. "Three of us came home. I have never stopped thinking about that."
Hindbad said nothing.
"I went back a fourth time," said Sinbad. "But not for a year. Come back tomorrow."
~ ~ ~
The Fourth Voyage: The Underground Kingd:
The fourth voyage, said Sinbad, is the one that taught me the most useful thing I know.
On the fourth voyage, I was captured.
Our ship was caught in a sudden storm — not a natural storm, but a storm that seemed to grow and grow and never end, driving us onto rocks, smashing the ship, scattering us into the water. I woke up on a beach with half a dozen survivors and no ship, no cargo, no supplies.
People found us on the beach. They were kind people — they brought us food and water and took us to their city, which was beautiful and strange. For several days everything was fine.
Then they brought us food.
I cannot explain it precisely. The food smelled wonderful. My companions ate it without hesitation.
Something in me refused.
The food made my companions hungry. The more they ate, the more they wanted, and the more they ate the less they could think about anything except eating. Within a week they could not carry on a proper conversation. Within two weeks they had forgotten their own names.
I pretended to eat. I hid the food when no one was watching. I stayed thin and quiet and sharp.
The people of the city fed my companions until they were enormous and then took them to a field and left them there. I never saw them again.
The same people found me. I was thin and clearly useless for what they wanted. They didn't know what to do with me. While they were deciding, I slipped away into the countryside.
I found a road. I followed it. I kept walking.
Eventually the road led me to a city where the people were normal and sensible and very surprised to see a half-starved sailor from Baghdad stumbling out of the wild lands to the east. I told my story to their king. He was fascinated. He gave me clothes and food and a horse.
In that city, no one used saddles. The king asked if I could make one — he had heard of saddles but never seen one. I built a saddle from wood and leather. The king was delighted. His nobles wanted saddles too. Within a month I was the most successful saddlemaker in the city, which is a sentence I had not expected to say about my life.
The king gave me his daughter in marriage.
I will stop there for a moment because Hindbad's expression had become quite startled.
Yes, said Sinbad. The king gave me his daughter in marriage. We were happy. I had a fine house and a good income and a wife I was very fond of, and I thought: this is where I will stay. The sea is done with me.
Then my wife got sick and died.
I was devastated. And then I discovered a custom of that city that I found deeply alarming: when a husband died, he was buried with his wife. When a wife died, she was buried with her husband. This was considered respectful. This was considered proper.
I was lowered into a burial chamber with a small lamp, a small amount of food and water, and my wife's body.
The lamp burned down. The food ran out. I waited in the dark for something to happen, because something had to happen — I refused to simply sit there and die in a hole while I was still perfectly capable of being alive.
I heard something. Scratching. Movement. An animal — some kind of wild creature that had found its way into the burial chambers through a crack in the rock.
I followed the sound. I crawled in the dark in the direction of the scratching for what felt like hours. Air moved against my face. Cold, fresh air.
I found the crack. I squeezed through it. I came out on the sea cliff in the light of morning.
There was a ship in the bay. I waved my shirt and shouted until they saw me.
Sinbad poured more tea. The useful thing I learned, he said, is this: always notice what you will not eat. Other people's customs are not always safe. A man who thinks clearly in a crisis will sometimes survive situations where a man who acts normally will not. And the crack in the wall is always there if you look hard enough in the dark.
Hindbad thought about this for a long time.
~ ~ ~
The Fifth Voyage: The Old Man of the Sea:
On the fifth voyage, said Sinbad, I made the worst mistake of my life.
I was kind to someone who did not deserve it.
I know that sounds wrong. Let me explain.
We landed on a beautiful island to take on fresh water. While exploring the island I found an old man sitting at the edge of a stream. He was very old, very frail, very thin. His clothes were rags. His eyes were large and sad.
"Can you help me?" he said. "I am very weak. I need to cross this stream. I cannot do it alone. If you carry me on your shoulders to the other side, I will be most grateful."
He was old. He was clearly harmless. I felt sorry for him.
I crouched down and he climbed on my back.
I crossed the stream.
I tried to let him down on the other side.
He would not let go.
His legs, which had appeared too weak to walk with, wrapped around my neck and chest with the strength of iron bands. His arms gripped my head. I pulled at his legs. I bent forward trying to shake him off. I spun around. He held on.
He was not going to let go. I understood this within the first few minutes.
He stayed on my back.
I had to walk with him on my back. I had to find food for him, eat food myself, sleep with him still holding on — he slept too, but his grip did not loosen even in sleep. If I fell down from exhaustion, he kicked my sides to make me get up.
This went on for weeks.
My shipmates, once they understood what had happened, were sorry for me and helpless to assist. He simply could not be removed.
One afternoon I found a patch of wild grapevines. The grapes were ripe. I squeezed juice from them into a large gourd shell and left it in the sun. After a few days, the juice had fermented into wine.
I drank some. I felt better. Warmer. Lighter.
He wanted some.
I gave him the gourd.
He drank deeply. His grip loosened. He laughed — a strange, rusty laugh — and drank more. The wine was strong and he was old and small. His arms went slack.
I threw him off my back. I ran. I did not look back.
My shipmates found me on the beach, thinner than when I had left, with marks on my neck where his legs had held. I told them what had happened.
The captain was not surprised. "The Old Man of the Sea," he said. "Everyone on these islands knows to avoid him. You are the first person I have ever heard of who got free."
We sailed away quickly, before he woke up.
The lesson of the fifth voyage, said Sinbad, is that kindness is good and important, but wisdom is knowing who actually needs your help and who is simply very good at appearing to need it. I should have asked more questions before crouching down. A genuine old man who cannot cross a stream cannot also hold on through weeks of struggling.
He shook his head. A very long month.
Hindbad, who had been holding his own neck sympathetically, nodded.
~ ~ ~
The Sixth Voyage: The River Under the Mo:
The sixth voyage began with a shipwreck, said Sinbad. This was not unusual at this point in my sailing career. What was unusual was where I ended up.
Our ship hit rocks in a bad storm and came apart. Those of us who survived made it to the shore of an island. The island had cliffs on all sides — high, steep, impossible to climb. The beach was narrow. There was no way off.
Let me tell you about this beach.
It was covered in things from shipwrecks. Decades of shipwrecks. Centuries of shipwrecks. Timbers, ropes, anchors, chests, barrels, rigging, broken masts. Every ship that had ever hit those rocks in those waters had ended up on that beach. And everyone who had survived those shipwrecks had also ended up on that beach.
The other survivors were there when I arrived. Old survivors. Very old. Too weak to do much besides point at things when I asked questions.
There were bones too. Many, many bones.
I understood the situation. I had arrived in a place people came to but did not leave.
I spent several days thinking and exploring. The island had a river that came out of the mountain, ran across the beach, and disappeared back into the mountain through a cave. A river has a source. A river has a place it goes. Rivers meet the sea eventually.
I built a raft from shipwreck timber. I loaded it with the best of the goods scattered on the beach — there was treasure there, from all those lost ships and lost merchants. I lashed myself to the raft so I would not fall off in the dark.
I pushed off into the cave.
The river pulled me in. It was dark. Completely dark. I could hear the water and feel the walls close on both sides and the ceiling low overhead. I could not see my hands.
I held on.
The river moved fast. I hit my head on the ceiling twice and decided to lie flat and stay flat. The raft bounced and spun. At some point — I do not know how long; it could have been hours — I fell asleep from sheer exhaustion.
I woke up in daylight.
I was drifting on a wide river in the sunshine. Trees on both banks. Birds in the trees. A road visible through the trees. A city on the horizon.
Farmers saw me floating past and pulled my raft to the bank. They asked where I had come from. I pointed at the mountain behind me.
They spoke to each other in low voices and gave me very concerned looks.
I was in the kingdom of Serendib, which is one of the most beautiful places in the world. The king heard about a merchant who had come through the mountain river — which was considered impossible — and summoned me to his court.
I told him my story. He gave me gifts. I gave him gifts from what I had salvaged.
I went home rich and grateful and absolutely certain I would never go to sea again.
You know how that turned out, said Sinbad with a smile. Come back tomorrow.
Hindbad shook his head slowly. "Six voyages. And you went a seventh time."
"The seventh time," said Sinbad, "was actually the king of Serendib's fault. But that is tomorrow's story."
~ ~ ~
A Night Off: Hindbad Asks Questions:
On the seventh night Sinbad did not tell a story.
Instead he said: you have been listening for six nights and eating my food every night, Hindbad, and I have done all the talking. Tonight I would like to know what you think.
Hindbad set down his cup. "About what?"
"About the voyages. About luck and skill and whether my fortune was earned or given."
Hindbad considered this. He was a thoughtful man, though he rarely got to show it because carrying loads did not leave much time for thinking.
"The whale island," he said. "That was luck. Bad luck first, then good luck. You did nothing wrong and almost died, and then you did nothing special and were found."
"That's fair," said Sinbad.
"The diamond valley — that was you. You thought of the turban and the meat and you did it. Another man would have sat down and despaired."
"Many men did exactly that. Their bones are in that valley."
"The giant's island was you again. The stakes. The rafts. You thought of those things and organized the others."
"I did. Though I lost eight men doing it."
"The fourth voyage — the burial chamber — that was mostly you. Listening in the dark. Following the crack."
"And luck that there was a crack," said Sinbad. "And luck that there was a ship in the bay."
"Everything is partly luck," said Hindbad. "But luck only helps if you are still alive and still thinking when it arrives. And you always were." He paused. "The old man of the sea — that was bad luck and a mistake."
"Definitely a mistake," said Sinbad. "I was too kind and not careful enough. I have regretted it."
"The river under the mountain — you made that path. You built the raft. You went into the dark."
"Yes."
Hindbad looked at his hands — the porter's hands, calloused and broad. "So the answer is: some luck, a great deal of thought, and a willingness to do the frightening thing when there is no other option."
"And the willingness to go back," said Sinbad. "After everything. To load the goods and find the ship and go again."
"Why?" said Hindbad. "After six voyages like those — why do it again?"
Sinbad was quiet for a long time.
"The sea asks you a question every voyage," he said finally. "Can you solve this? Can you survive this? Can you find the way out of this particular impossible situation? Most men never hear that question. Most men live and die in one city on one street and never know what they are capable of."
"And you needed to know."
"I could not rest until I did," said Sinbad. "I still cannot explain it better than that."
Hindbad nodded slowly. He thought he understood.
"Tomorrow," said Sinbad, "the seventh voyage. And then you will know the whole story."
~ ~ ~
The Seventh Voyage: The King's Letter:
After the sixth voyage I was truly done, said Sinbad. I was home. I was rich. I was comfortable. I had been shipwrecked more times than I could count on one hand. I was finished with the sea.
Then the Caliph of Baghdad summoned me.
The Caliph had heard of the kingdom of Serendib — its beauty, its wealth, the kindness of its king. He wanted to send a letter and gifts to the king of Serendib. He needed someone who knew the way.
I knew the way.
I tried to explain that I was retired. The Caliph looked at me. When the Caliph of Baghdad looks at you in a particular way, you pack your bags.
I packed my bags.
The voyage to Serendib was actually fine. No storms. No shipwrecks. No strange islands. I delivered the letter and the gifts to the king, he received them with great joy, and I prepared to sail home. Easy. Done.
On the way home, pirates found us.
Our ship was large and well-armed, but the pirates had three ships and they attacked from three sides at once. Some of my companions were killed. The rest of us were taken prisoner.
The pirates brought us to an island and sold us at a market. I was purchased by an ivory merchant named Khasib, who was a kind enough master as masters go — he fed me well and did not beat me, which I will acknowledge as the minimum possible decency but also better than the alternative.
The merchant had a problem. The elephants in this region had been killing his hunters. He needed someone to shoot the elephants from a tree — high enough to be safe, skilled enough to aim. He had run out of local men willing to do it.
I was desperate enough to try. I climbed trees and I aimed carefully and I was a reasonable shot, which I had not known about myself until I absolutely needed to know it.
The elephants noticed me.
This is the part of the story that I find most remarkable.
One morning an enormous old elephant came to the base of my tree and waited. When I climbed down, it did not attack me. It walked away and looked back.
I followed it. I followed it for an hour through the jungle, which was either very wise or very foolish, and I genuinely did not know which. It led me to a place in the hills where the bones of hundreds of elephants lay piled — the elephants' graveyard, the place they went to die.
There was more ivory in that valley than a hundred merchants could carry.
The elephant looked at me. I believe it was saying: take the ivory. Stop killing us. There is enough here.
I went back to Khasib and told him what I had found. He brought everyone to the graveyard. We loaded ship after ship with ivory. It took weeks. Khasib was so grateful that he freed me, paid me a large share of the ivory's value, and sailed me home personally.
I arrived in Baghdad the second time with enough wealth to live on for the rest of my life, with money left over to give away.
Sinbad looked at Hindbad across the long table. "That was the seventh voyage. That was the last voyage."
"The elephants," said Hindbad. "They showed you the way."
"They trusted me with it," said Sinbad. "After everything I had done." He shook his head. "I think about them often."
He refilled Hindbad's cup for the final time. "Now you have heard the whole story. Does my fortune seem easily come by?"
Hindbad looked at the fine house around him, the carpets, the food, the lamps burning late into the night, and then he looked at Sinbad — the bright eyes, the white hair, the hands that had gripped a piece of meat while an eagle lifted him out of a pit and clung to the leg of the largest bird in the world.
"No," he said. "It does not."
Sinbad smiled. "Then we understand each other."
He gave Hindbad a purse of gold when he left. More than a porter earned in a month. More than he expected.
Hindbad walked home slowly, thinking about the sea, thinking about questions the world asks you that you never hear if you never go to look for them.
He went home to his family and his work and his ordinary life.
But for the rest of his life, whenever his load felt heavy and the sun felt too hot and the world felt unfair, he thought about a man clinging to an eagle's foot while a valley of diamonds spun far below him, and he thought about what it cost to deserve the fine house at the end of the story.
And he picked up his load.
And he walked on.
~ ~ ~
Back to the Beginning:
Let me start again, said Sinbad, more slowly this time.
You have heard the shape of the seven voyages. But the shape of a thing and the feel of it are different. Tonight I will tell you what it was actually like to be twenty-three years old and broke and boarding a ship for the first time.
I was terrified.
I would not have admitted this then. Young men rarely admit to being terrified because being terrified feels like weakness and weakness feels like something to be ashamed of. I understand now that being terrified is simply information — it means you are about to do something that matters.
I stood at the dock in Basra with my bales of fabric and my small sacks of spice and I looked at the ship. It was old. The wood was dark with water and salt. The crew were hard-faced men who did not look at passengers with much warmth.
The captain was a man named Ibrahim, and he ran his ship well and treated us fairly, and I will always be grateful to him because the first captain you sail with sets your idea of what a captain is. If he had been cruel or incompetent, I might have been afraid of every ship for the rest of my life.
The sea on that first day was calm and blue and vast and I thought: I understand why men love this. The ship moved through the water with a sound like breathing. The sails were full. The horizon was endless.
After three weeks we stopped at islands I did not know the names of. Strange trees, strange birds, strange fruits that tasted nothing like anything in Baghdad. I bought things cheaply at one island and sold them for more at the next. I was learning how trade worked on the sea — that distance is money, that what is common in one place is rare in another, that patience and attention are worth more than luck.
I was good at this. I was surprised to discover I was good at it.
Then we stopped at what looked like an island and I fell asleep on the grass and the island moved.
You already know this part. But what I have not told you is what it felt like to be in the water afterward.
Cold. The ocean is much colder than it looks from a ship's deck. The whale had gone down and the water it displaced had churned everything into foam and I was in the middle of it with no direction and no idea which way was the ship.
I found the washtub not because I was clever but because it bumped into my arm.
I held on. I shut my eyes and held on and did not think about what was below me in all that dark water, which is a very important skill that I recommend developing if you plan to go to sea.
Two days.
Two days on that washtub. The sun was very hot. I had nothing to drink. I talked to myself to stay awake during the second night, because I was afraid that if I fell asleep I would let go.
And then the island appeared. And the horses. And King Mihrjan, who asked no suspicious questions and gave me a job and treated me as a person rather than a problem.
The kindness of strangers, said Sinbad, is a real thing. I have encountered cruelty and I have encountered kindness, and I cannot tell you which I have encountered more. But the kindness of strangers is what makes the world crossable. Never forget to be kind to the stranger who washes up on your shore.
He looked at Hindbad. "That is why I invited you in, when I heard you on my steps. You reminded me of someone I used to be."
Hindbad was quiet for a moment. "I didn't expect that," he said.
"Most things worth having are unexpected," said Sinbad. "Come back tomorrow."
~ ~ ~
The Ship That Did Not Wait:
Here is something nobody tells you about going to sea, said Sinbad.
Ships do not wait.
This seems obvious. But it is the kind of obvious thing that you do not fully understand until a ship fails to wait for you and you are standing on an island watching the sails disappear.
On the second voyage, the island we stopped at was genuinely beautiful. There was a stream with cold water and flat rocks to sit on and shade from old trees. Several of us went ashore to rest. The captain announced we would leave after the midday meal.
I found my shady spot and my book and I was reading and then I was asleep, which I have mentioned before. But what I have not told you is that I woke up not all at once but slowly, gradually, the way you wake when no one is shaking you, when the sun has just moved and the light has changed.
For a moment, half-asleep, I thought: what a pleasant afternoon.
Then I thought: it is afternoon.
Then I sat up and looked at the shore.
The ship was a small shape on the horizon. Still visible. Getting smaller.
I ran to the beach and shouted. I waved my arms. I ran along the shore as if I could somehow chase it.
The ship got smaller.
I sat down on the beach.
Here is what I want you to understand about that moment: the worst part was not fear. Fear came later. The worst part was embarrassment. I had done this. I had fallen asleep knowing we were leaving after the midday meal. I was not a careless person — I thought of myself as careful and smart and good at paying attention. And I had simply fallen asleep and let the ship leave without me.
The embarrassment was followed by fear, and then by the practical question: what do I actually do now?
I could sit here and feel sorry for myself. That was one option.
I could explore the island and understand my situation. That was another.
I explored.
That is how I found the dome. That is how I found the Roc.
The thing about terrible situations is that they force you to pay attention in a way you do not when things are going well. When I was safe on the ship, reading my book in the afternoon, I was not thinking very hard about anything. When I was stranded on the island alone, I noticed every single thing — the height of the trees, the direction of the wind, the shape of that white dome, the shadow that fell from the sky.
Necessity sharpens the mind considerably.
I am not saying it is good to be shipwrecked. It is not good to be shipwrecked. But I will say that every time I found myself in a desperate situation, I discovered something about myself that I did not know before.
On the whale island, I discovered I could endure two days alone in the open ocean without giving up.
In the diamond valley, I discovered I could think creatively when I was frightened.
On the giant's island, I discovered I could organize frightened people into doing something brave.
None of these things would I have learned in a comfortable house in Baghdad.
Sinbad paused. The candles had burned low. Somewhere in the house a door closed softly.
"I am not saying," he said carefully, "that you should go looking for disasters. I am saying that if you find yourself in one, it will show you things about yourself that nothing else will. And those things are worth knowing."
Hindbad looked at his hands in the dim light.
"What did you discover," he asked, "that you wish you hadn't?"
Sinbad was quiet for a long moment.
"That I can leave people behind," he said, "when leaving them is the only way to survive. I have never decided if that is strength or cowardice. Perhaps it is neither. Perhaps it is simply what survival requires."
He blew out the nearest candle.
"Come back tomorrow."
~ ~ ~
What the Giant Taught Me:
I have thought about the giant many times, said Sinbad.
Not the terror of him — though that was real — but the strangeness of what he was. He was not evil, exactly. He was not cruel in the way that a person who knows what cruelty is can be cruel. He came to the beach and he looked at us and he chose the largest man and he ate him.
He did this the same way a lion does it. With complete calm. Without malice. It was simply what he did.
This did not make it better. I want to be clear about that. Eight of my companions died because of him. I do not look back on those days with anything except grief and anger.
But here is the thought that has stayed with me: he was not doing something wrong, from his own perspective. We were on his island. He was hungry. We were available.
We were the strangers who arrived in a place where we were not welcome and where we were not prepared for what lived there.
Now. I am not saying we deserved what happened to us. No one deserves to be eaten by a giant.
What I am saying is that every sea voyage, every journey into a place you do not know, requires you to hold in your head the possibility that you are the stranger, and that the rules of the strange place may be entirely different from the rules of your home, and that things that seem safe or obvious or normal at home may not be safe or obvious or normal there.
The fourth voyage, in the city with the strange food — I survived because something in me refused to eat without thinking first. My companions ate because the food smelled good and they were hungry and eating is what you do when you are hungry. I asked myself: why is this food here? Who prepared it? Why are these people being so generous to strangers?
Questions. Just questions. Questions kept me alive.
Ask questions, said Sinbad. Always ask questions. When something seems too convenient — a meal that appears, a path that opens, a helpful stranger — ask why. Not rudely. Not suspiciously. But ask.
Hindbad was quiet.
"Did you ask questions," he said, "before you picked up the Old Man of the Sea?"
Sinbad laughed, a short rueful sound. "No. I did not. He looked pitiful and I felt sorry for him and I crouched down before I thought. Which is why I ended up with him on my back for a month." He shook his head. "I was not consistent. No one is consistent. You learn a lesson and then you forget it in the moment when it counts, and then you learn it again more painfully."
"Do you ever stop making mistakes?"
"I am ninety years old and very rich," said Sinbad, "and I still make mistakes. The difference is that most of them now happen in a comfortable house rather than on a life raft in the middle of the ocean."
He smiled. "That is probably the best outcome a person can hope for."
~ ~ ~
The City Under the Mountain Sky:
When I woke up on the other side of the mountain river, said Sinbad, I did not know where I was.
I knew I was alive. I knew there was sunlight. I knew the raft was still under me and still moving, which meant the river was still carrying me somewhere.
I lay flat on the raft and looked at the sky and did not move for quite a long time.
Eventually I sat up.
The river was wide and slow and the banks were covered in tall green trees. Birds called from the branches — birds I did not recognize, with brighter colors than Baghdad birds. The air smelled different. Wet and green and alive.
I had gone into a mountain through a cave in darkness and come out into a different country. I am a practical man and I am not given to thinking in symbols and meanings, but I will admit that lying on that raft in that new light, I thought: this is what it feels like to be given another chance.
The farmers who pulled my raft to shore were kind and curious. They gathered around me asking questions in a language I did not know, pointing at the mountain, looking very concerned. I answered in Arabic and they understood none of it. I pointed at the mountain. They spoke to each other urgently.
Eventually someone arrived who spoke a little Persian, which I spoke a little of, and between us we managed to communicate the basic facts: I had come through the mountain river from the other side. This was, apparently, something no one had done before.
They gave me food and dry clothes and a place to sleep. In the morning they took me to the city.
The city of Serendib was the most beautiful city I had ever seen. Not the largest — Baghdad was larger, and Basra, and the cities of Persia. But beautiful in a particular way, as if the people who built it had thought carefully about what would make it pleasant to be in rather than simply functional. Trees in the streets. Fountains at every corner. Colored tiles on the walls.
The king received me in a hall open on three sides to gardens full of flowers. He was a man of middle age, serious and warm, with the kind of face that listens when you speak.
I told him my story. He listened to all of it without interrupting.
When I finished, he said: "You have survived things that should not be survivable. I would like to hear why you think that is."
I thought about it. "Because I kept thinking," I said. "And because I was lucky. Both things together."
"Most men," he said, "when they are frightened, stop thinking. You did not."
"I was frightened every time," I said. "I was terrified. But terror is just energy that hasn't been pointed anywhere yet. I tried to point it somewhere useful."
The king nodded as if I had said something he already knew but was pleased to hear someone else say.
He kept me in Serendib for three months. He showed me the island, which was as beautiful as the city. He introduced me to his court, his scholars, his astronomers who charted the stars from a tower at the edge of the sea.
When I left, he gave me more gifts than the ship could comfortably carry.
I arrived home richer than I had ever been, said Sinbad. And I was certain — truly certain, this time — that I was finished with the sea.
He was quiet for a moment.
"And then came the letter," said Hindbad.
"And then came the letter," said Sinbad.
~ ~ ~
The Letter the Caliph Sent:
The Caliph of Baghdad was a man who got what he wanted.
This is not a criticism. The Caliph was, on the whole, a good ruler who wanted reasonable things and usually got them through fair means. But when he wanted something, he wanted it, and the matter of the king of Serendib had been on his mind for months.
"I have heard of this king," the Caliph said to me, when I stood before him in the great hall. "I have heard of his kindness and his beautiful island and his wisdom. I would like to send him greetings. And gifts."
"A fine idea," I said carefully. "I'm sure one of your experienced ambassadors could make the voyage."
The Caliph looked at me. "You know the way."
"I am retired," I said. "I have been on seven — well, six at that point — I have been on six extremely eventful voyages, and I have come home from all of them, and I feel that pressing my luck a seventh time is unwise."
The Caliph continued to look at me.
"I will of course be honored to serve," I said.
The voyage out was good. The sea was kind, the winds were favorable, and I arrived at Serendib to find the king exactly as I had left him — warm, thoughtful, pleased to see me, delighted by the Caliph's letter and gifts.
I stayed for a week. I enjoyed every moment of it. The gardens, the fountains, the tower where the astronomers watched the stars. The king fed me excellent food and we talked long into the evenings about everything — trade, philosophy, the differences between Baghdad and Serendib, the strange things the sea brought to both of our shores.
On the last evening he gave me his reply to the Caliph's letter, sealed and wrapped, and more gifts for the Caliph: rubies, fine cloth, a carved sandalwood box with a message inside.
I said goodbye. I boarded my ship. We set sail.
Three days later, pirates.
They came from behind an island, three ships to our one, and they hit us from three sides at once. Our crew fought. Two of the pirates' ships had more men than ours. The third came in from the far side while we were occupied with the first two.
It was over very quickly.
The pirates were not particularly cruel — they wanted cargo and captives, not a fight. They took us alive, which at the time I was grateful for and later came to have more complicated feelings about.
They sailed us to an island market and sold us.
Standing in a market being sold is a very particular kind of humiliation. I had been shipwrecked and stranded and lost at sea, but I had never been a piece of goods on a table before. The pirates described our skills — this one is strong, this one is a navigator, this one is a merchant from Baghdad with useful knowledge of trade routes.
They sold me to a man named Khasib. He bought several of us.
He was, as I said, a reasonable master. He did not beat us. He fed us adequately. He explained what he needed from us clearly and then let us get on with it.
What he needed was someone willing to sit in a tree and shoot elephants.
He explained this to me as if it were an entirely ordinary thing to ask.
I thought about my options. I did not have many.
"I can shoot," I said.
~ ~ ~
The Elephant and the Secret Valley:
I learned to hunt elephants, said Sinbad, out of necessity. I want to be honest that I am not proud of this. They are intelligent animals — the most intelligent I have ever encountered other than people, and sometimes more trustworthy than people.
Khasib's hunters had been killed by elephants protecting their young. He needed someone to shoot from high in the trees, where the elephants could not reach. He had run out of local men willing to try.
I was not a man with choices.
I climbed trees. I learned to be very quiet and very still for long periods, which is a skill I had not needed before. I learned to read the forest — which sounds told you an elephant was nearby, which sounds meant it was agitated, which meant it was calm. I learned which trees were strong enough to hold a person and also high enough to be safe.
I shot from those trees for several weeks.
Then the elephants started watching me.
Not in the way of an animal that is angry or afraid. In the way of an animal that is figuring something out. They would stand at the base of my tree and look up. Not charging. Not threatening. Just looking.
I found this more unnerving than if they had charged.
One morning the old elephant came.
I had seen it before — huge, ancient, with worn tusks and eyes that looked at you with a weight that made you feel observed rather than simply looked at. It stood under my tree and waited. When I climbed down, it did not move. When I took a step, it turned and walked away and looked back.
I want to tell you that I was brave and confident when I followed it. I was not. My hands were shaking. Every part of my mind that is reasonable was saying: do not follow an elephant through a jungle. Every part of me that was desperate and had no other ideas said: follow the elephant.
The desperate part won.
We walked for an hour. The elephant moved at a pace I could keep up with, which I noticed — it was not running from me or marching ahead, it was walking at a person's pace, checking that I was still following.
The valley it led me to was in a fold of hills that you would never find unless you were led to it. The bones lay thick across the valley floor — hundreds of them, maybe thousands. Skulls. Ribs. Enormous curved tusks, creamy white, worth a king's ransom each.
The old elephant stopped at the edge of the valley. It turned its head and looked at me.
I understood.
The elephants knew I was after ivory. They had brought me to more ivory than I could ever take, and they had done it to show me: here is what you want. Take it. It is ours to give. Stop hunting us.
I stood at the edge of that valley for a long time.
Then I went back to Khasib and told him what I had found.
His face when he understood what I was describing — a valley full of tusks, given freely, more than a hundred hunters could have gathered in a lifetime — was a remarkable thing to see.
"You followed an elephant," he said slowly.
"I did."
"Into the jungle."
"Yes."
He stared at me. "Why?"
I thought about it. "Because it asked me to," I said. "And because it seemed to have something to say."
Khasib looked at me as if he was not entirely sure I was sane. But he came to the valley. He saw it. He filled his ships.
He freed me.
~ ~ ~
The Long Way Home:
There is a difference between going somewhere and coming home, said Sinbad.
Going somewhere, you are full of what might happen. You do not know the place. You do not know the people. Every morning is a mystery waiting to be opened. Even on the terrible voyages — even on the islands where things went wrong in every direction — there was a quality of aliveness in not knowing what the day would bring.
Coming home, you know what you will find. Your house. Your friends. The streets you grew up on. The smell of Baghdad in the morning — bread and horses and river water and the ten thousand different things that make up the smell of a city you have known all your life.
I have come home six times from sea voyages. Each time was different.
The first time, I was relieved. Just that — pure relief, like putting down a very heavy thing. I had survived. I was back. The world I knew still existed.
The second time, I was grateful. I had been to the bottom of a valley of diamonds and the top of a cliff carried by an eagle and I had seen things no one in Baghdad had seen, and I was grateful to be back among people who understood what I was saying when I spoke.
The third time, I was grieving. Eight men had died. I could not celebrate coming home when eight men had not come home.
The fourth time, I was different. I had been a slave in a hole underground and I had crawled out in darkness and found a crack in the wall and a ship in the bay, and when I walked into Baghdad I felt as if I had been given the city as a gift. I noticed it more. The light on the river. The way the markets smelled. Children playing in the courtyards.
The fifth time, I was angry. The Old Man of the Sea had taken weeks of my life, weeks I will never have back, and I was home but I was still angry at myself for the mistake that had cost them.
The sixth time, through the mountain river, I came home quietly. I did not celebrate. I sat in my house by myself for three days and thought about the beach covered in shipwreck bones and the men who had ended their lives there.
The seventh time — the last time — I came home a free man who had been a slave and a hunter and an interpreter between an old elephant and a frightened merchant, and I had the Caliph's reply from the king of Serendib in my bag, and Khasib's generous payment in my purse, and I walked through the gates of Baghdad knowing I was home for the last time.
There was no triumph in it. Not exactly. More like a completion. Like a long sentence that has finally reached its end.
I bought back my father's house. I hired good people to run it well. I invited my friends and fed them generously. I sat in the courtyard on warm evenings and listened to the sounds of the city.
Hindbad had been listening with his chin in his hand.
"Do you miss it?" he asked. "The sea."
Sinbad considered this carefully. "I miss the question it asks," he said finally. "The question of what you're capable of. But I am old now, and I have answered that question often enough. It is enough to remember the answers."
"What were the answers?"
"More than I expected," said Sinbad. "Every time, more than I expected."
~ ~ ~
What Wealth Actually Costs:
I want to tell you something about this house, said Sinbad.
Hindbad looked around at the carpets and the lamps and the carved furniture.
"Every fine thing in it cost something it does not look like it cost," said Sinbad. "The carpets came from trade goods bought with money earned in the diamond valley. The diamond valley was a pit I was dropped into on the back of an enormous bird after being abandoned on an island. The lamps were bought with profit from the sixth voyage's salvage. The sixth voyage ended with me floating through a mountain in the dark on a raft I built from shipwreck timber. The chair you are sitting on was purchased the year I came home from the ivory valley. The ivory valley was shown to me by an elephant who had watched me kill other elephants for weeks and then decided to offer me a better option."
He paused. "Nothing in this house cost money, exactly. Everything in it cost time and fear and the willingness to keep going past the point where stopping felt completely reasonable."
Hindbad looked at the chair differently.
"I am telling you this," said Sinbad, "not to make you feel that your work is less than mine. A porter who carries loads honestly and comes home to his family every evening and feeds his children is doing something real and valuable and worthy of respect. I am not looking down at you from this chair."
"Why are you telling me, then?"
"Because you sat on my steps and said the world was unfair. And you were not wrong. The world is not fair — rich men have easier lives than poor men in a dozen obvious ways, and that is genuinely unjust in many of those ways. I do not argue with that."
He leaned forward. "But you said it as if there was nothing to be done. As if the distance between your steps and my front door was fixed and permanent and uncrossable. And that part I would push back on."
"You had advantages I don't have," said Hindbad. "You could afford trade goods."
"I sold my furniture," said Sinbad. "I had a table and three chairs and some bed linens and a cooking pot. That is all I had when I first went to sea. It was not much."
"But you were willing to go," said Hindbad. "Not everyone can do that."
"True," said Sinbad. "Not everyone can leave what they know. And I am not saying everyone should. I am saying — " He paused, thinking. "I am saying that the distance between where you are and where you want to be is made up of choices and work and some luck, and the choices and work are the part you can affect."
He sat back. "That is all. It is not a comfortable answer. I know it is not comfortable. The world should be more fair and I am an old man who got lucky seven times, and if I had died on the whale island none of this would exist and no one would be telling inspiring stories about Sinbad the Sailor."
"I know," said Hindbad.
"But I didn't die on the whale island," said Sinbad. "And the choices I made after I was in the water — those were mine."
Hindbad was quiet for a while. "I will think about that," he said.
"That," said Sinbad, "is all I can ask."
~ ~ ~
The King of Serendib Remembers:
Let me tell you about the king of Serendib, said Sinbad, because he is worth knowing.
His name was Maharaj, and he had ruled his island for thirty years. He was a fair king — his people were well-fed and safe, his courts handled disputes honestly, his roads were kept clear of bandits. He was not remarkable in the way of conquerors or warriors. He was remarkable in the way of someone who does ordinary things extraordinarily well.
When I arrived the first time, through the mountain river, half-drowned and barely able to walk, he asked me how I had gotten there.
I told him.
He asked me what it had felt like to go into the cave in the dark.
I told him that too — the cold water, the sound of the walls close on both sides, the way I had kept my hands out to feel for obstacles.
"Were you afraid?" he asked.
"Terrified," I said.
"What made you go in anyway?"
I thought about it. "The beach," I said. "The beach was worse. I knew what the beach would eventually be. The river at least went somewhere."
He nodded slowly. He had the expression of someone filing something away.
Later, when I was rested and fed and wearing clean clothes and sitting in his garden, he came to find me.
"I have ruled this island for thirty years," he said. "I have never been to sea. I was born here, and here I will die. I have never had the experience of going into the cave because the beach was worse."
"It is not an experience I recommend," I said.
"No," he said. "But you describe it as something that made you capable of going into the next cave, and the one after that. A skill acquired through necessity."
"I suppose so."
"That is something I cannot give my children," he said. "Security is good. Safety is good. But a person who has never been in a cave has never had to find out if they have the courage to go through the dark."
He was quiet for a moment. "I think about this when I look at my sons. They are kind boys. They are educated. They have never been hungry or afraid."
"That is not entirely a problem," I said.
"No," he agreed. "But it is a little bit of one."
I thought about this conversation for a long time on the voyage home. I thought about my own father, who had left me money that I wasted, and about what he might have done differently. Probably nothing. Probably I needed to waste the money in order to be desperate enough to go to sea, and the sea was what made me who I am.
Perhaps, said Sinbad, some caves have to be chosen. And some are simply the only door left open.
He looked at Hindbad. "Did you choose your work?"
"No," said Hindbad. "I had a family to feed."
"And did it make you capable?"
Hindbad thought about it. About the loads he had carried, the distances, the heat, the weight. About what his body could do now that it could not do when he was young.
"Yes," he said slowly. "I suppose it did."
~ ~ ~
The Shape of the Sea:
Here is something I learned about the sea that I did not expect, said Sinbad.
The sea changes shape.
Not the water itself — the ocean does not have a fixed outline any more than a river does. I mean the sea changes what it looks like from a ship's deck depending on the weather and the time of year and the part of it you are in. The sea I sailed in summer was blue-green and clear and you could see fish moving twenty feet below the surface. The sea in winter storms was gray-black and opaque and moved like something alive and angry. The sea in the early morning, before the sun was fully up, had a particular silver quality that made it look like hammered metal.
I never got tired of looking at it.
This surprises people. After everything the sea did to me — the storms, the shipwrecks, the islands, the whale, the pirates, everything — you would expect me to hate it. I do not hate it. I am no longer able to sail on it, at my age, and I miss it.
Not the danger. I do not miss nearly dying. But the quality of the sea — its size, its aliveness, its absolute indifference to whether I lived or died — that I miss.
The sea does not care about you. It does not wish you well or ill. It simply is. It is enormous and old and it will be here long after every person on every ship has returned to the earth.
There is something restful about that, when you accept it.
The worst sailors I met were the ones who took the sea personally. When a storm came they said: why is this happening to me? When the wind died they said: why has it abandoned us? The sea was not punishing them and the sea had not abandoned them — the sea was simply being the sea.
The best sailors I met accepted this and worked with it rather than against it. The weather is doing this — what can we do now? The wind is from this direction — what does that mean for our course?
Questions again, said Sinbad. Always questions.
The sea is also generous, when you are paying attention. It gives you fish if you know where to look. It gives you current if you know how to read it. It gives you wind if you set your sails right. It is not your enemy. It is simply a very large environment with its own rules, and the rules can be learned.
The same is true of most things in life that seem frightening from the outside.
Hindbad had stopped eating and was simply listening.
"You talk about the sea the way you talk about a person," he said.
"I know," said Sinbad. "After seven voyages, it felt like one to me. Strange and difficult and occasionally dangerous, but a person you could come to know."
He smiled. "I have known worse people."
Hindbad laughed. It was the first time he had laughed in several nights.
"Good," said Sinbad. "You needed that."
~ ~ ~
What Was Lost:
There are things I have not told you, said Sinbad.
Not because I wanted to hide them. Because they are heavy things and I was waiting until we knew each other well enough for heavy things.
On the third voyage, eight men died. I told you that. I was the one who organized the attack on the giant — the stakes, the fire. I was the one who decided we could not wait any longer to act. If I had decided differently, perhaps some of those men would have survived. If I had decided differently, perhaps none of us would have.
I do not know. I have never known.
I know that I made the choice I thought was right with the information I had at the time. I know that eight men went into that sea when the giant threw the boulders and did not come out. I know their names. I have not forgotten a single one of them.
Yusuf, the carpenter from Basra. Hamid, who had a daughter he was saving money for. Tariq, who was the best cook on any ship I ever sailed. Five others. I knew all of them.
On the second raft, the one that was not mine, there were six men. The boulder hit them directly. There was nothing left of the raft or the men to retrieve.
I held this for a long time before I could say it to anyone. I still hold it. A wealthy man in a fine house in Baghdad carries the names of eight men who followed his plan and died for it.
This is what wealth actually costs, Hindbad. Not the storms. Not the fear. The weight of the names.
Hindbad was very still.
"I'm sorry," he said.
"Thank you," said Sinbad. "I do not often hear that." He was quiet for a moment. "I funded their families when I returned. It was not enough. It could never be enough. But it was something I could do, and I did it."
He looked at the lamp. "I went back to sea again, on the fourth voyage, partly because I could not sit in my comfortable house with those names. The sea asked me questions that distracted me from the ones I could not answer."
He looked at Hindbad. "Do you understand that?"
Hindbad thought about it. He thought about the days after his own father died, when he had taken every difficult job he could find — the heaviest loads, the longest distances — because moving was easier than sitting still.
"Yes," he said. "I understand that."
"I thought you might," said Sinbad.
They sat together in the quiet for a while, which was its own kind of conversation.
~ ~ ~
The Merchants He Met:
Let me tell you about the people I met, said Sinbad. Not the kings and the monsters and the strange customs — you have heard plenty of that. Let me tell you about the ordinary people.
On the first voyage, on King Mihrjan's island, there was a spice merchant named Farouk who had been trading on those routes for forty years. He knew every island, every current, every reliable captain on the eastern sea. He had a small shop near the harbor where he sorted and packaged his goods in the evenings, and he let me sit with him and ask questions.
I learned more from Farouk in three months than I had learned in twenty-three years in Baghdad. He taught me which spices were worth what, and why, and how to tell the difference between fresh and old, and how to negotiate without appearing to negotiate. He taught me that most successful trade is just careful observation: knowing what something costs here and what it sells for there, and finding a reliable way to move it between the two places.
"The sea is just a road," said Farouk. "A difficult one. But still a road."
He was the first person who made me see my voyages as trade rather than adventure. That shift in perspective changed how I did everything.
On the fourth voyage, in the city with the strange food, there was an old woman who spoke to me in a language I mostly didn't understand, but who clearly understood that I was not eating the food I was supposed to eat and was choosing not to say anything about it. She brought me plain bread and clean water every evening, left at the threshold of my room.
I never found out who she was. I never learned her name. She saved my life with bread and water and silence.
On the sixth voyage, on the beach of shipwrecks, there was a man who had been there for eleven years. He had survived eleven years on a beach that was supposed to be a place you died in. He had built a shelter, found a freshwater spring, learned which plants were edible, organized the other survivors into something like a community. When I arrived and started talking about the river in the mountain, he was the first person who listened carefully.
"The river goes somewhere," I said.
"It goes underground," he said. "Into the dark."
"Somewhere," I said. "Eventually it goes somewhere."
He helped me build the raft.
He was too old and too weak to go through the river himself. He knew this. He helped me build it anyway.
His name was Daoud. I got home. I do not know if he is still on that beach or if anyone ever went back for him. I have thought about going back. I have thought about it many times.
I did not go.
Sinbad was quiet for a moment.
"The people who helped you," said Hindbad. "Did you ever repay them?"
"Farouk I sent goods to, from Baghdad, for years. The bread woman — I never found her again. Daoud — I sent a ship to that beach, three years later. The beach was empty. I choose to believe he found his own way out."
"Do you believe it?"
"I choose to," said Sinbad. "There are some things you choose to believe because the alternative is heavier than you can carry."
He refilled their cups.
"But the merchants," he said. "The ordinary working people on those islands, trading their goods and feeding their families and knowing every current on the eastern sea — they are the ones who made the voyages possible. Not the adventures. The quiet, reliable, useful people."
He looked at Hindbad steadily. "Not unlike a porter who shows up every morning and carries what needs to be carried."
~ ~ ~
The Last Thing I Learned:
On the seventh voyage, said Sinbad, after Khasib freed me, I had a long journey home.
Khasib sailed me to the nearest large port, which was still several weeks from Baghdad by land. He gave me money for the journey and enough ivory value in trade goods to make me comfortable, and he shook my hand and said: I have never met a man like you, and I hope my path crosses yours again.
It has not. I hope he is well.
I traveled home slowly through places I had not seen before, trading as I went, buying and selling small things, sleeping in caravanserais with merchants from a dozen different countries. I spoke Arabic and some Persian and a little of the eastern island languages and enough bad Turkish to get by, and I ate what was offered and slept in whatever bed was available and I was happy.
Just happy. Quietly, ordinarily happy.
Not because of the ivory or the money or the thought of coming home. Happy because I was a person moving through the world in a useful way, talking to people about useful things, buying a bolt of red cloth in one town and selling it in the next town where red cloth was wanted.
The road from the sea to Baghdad took six weeks. I spent six weeks being a perfectly ordinary traveling merchant, and it was one of the best six weeks of my life.
I had spent my voyages learning to survive extraordinary things. The last thing I learned was how to be content with ordinary ones.
This is the hardest thing, said Sinbad. Not the Roc. Not the giant. Not the Old Man of the Sea. The hardest thing is to be happy when nothing remarkable is happening, when every day is similar to the last, when no eagle is lifting you from a pit and no river is carrying you through a mountain.
Most of life is ordinary. You wake up. You do your work. You eat your food. You sleep. The adventures are few and far between and mostly shorter than they seem in the telling.
The question is whether you can be grateful for the ordinary things. The bread. The bed. The clean water. The road that is just a road, not a test or an adventure, just a path from one place to another.
I could not see this when I was young and broke and angry. When you have nothing, ordinary feels like deprivation. Once I had seen enough extraordinary things — once I had been in enough valleys of diamonds and burial chambers and giant-haunted islands — ordinary started to look different.
Ordinary meant safe. Ordinary meant alive. Ordinary meant home.
Hindbad was quiet.
"I think I understand," he said. "But I think it is easier to appreciate ordinary when you have chosen it. When it is all you have, it feels like no choice at all."
"Yes," said Sinbad. "You are right. I cannot argue with that." He paused. "What I can say is that the ordinary things in your life — your family, your work, the streets of Baghdad, the weight on your shoulders that tells you you are useful — those things are real and worth having. Even when you did not choose them."
Hindbad thought about his children's faces when he came home in the evening.
"Even then," he said.
"Even then," agreed Sinbad.
~ ~ ~
Morning at the Sailor's House:
On the last morning Hindbad arrived early, before the other guests.
He knocked at the gate, not sure if it was too early. A servant opened it immediately — servants in a house like Sinbad's were always awake before the household needed them — and brought him into the garden.
The garden was cool in the early morning, the air still holding the night's quiet. A fountain ran at the center. Doves called from somewhere in the old fig tree.
Sinbad was already there, sitting in a low chair with a glass of mint tea, watching the light change on the fountain.
He looked smaller in the morning light, somehow. Older. Just an old man in a garden, with a teacup.
Then he looked up and smiled and was Sinbad again.
"You came early," he said.
"I wanted to ask you something before the others arrive," said Hindbad. "Before the story ends properly."
A servant brought Hindbad tea. He sat down across from Sinbad.
"Ask," said Sinbad.
"Was it worth it?" said Hindbad. "All of it — the voyages, the danger, the men you lost, the years of your life on ships and islands and caves. Was this —" He gestured at the house, the garden, the fountain. "Was this worth all of that?"
Sinbad held his teacup in both hands and looked at the water.
"I cannot answer for the men I lost," he said. "That is not mine to answer. For them, clearly, no — they paid a price that was not fair and did not lead to any reward for themselves. I carry that."
He paused. "For me — yes. But not because of the house. This house would mean nothing if it were all I had to show for seventy years. It would just be a building."
"What does it mean, then?"
"It means I know what I am capable of," said Sinbad. "I know that I can think clearly when I am frightened. I know that I can endure being alone and lost and hungry and humiliated and still find the way out. I know that when a door closes, I will look for the crack in the wall. I know that when an elephant shows me something, I should follow."
He smiled. "I know the names of more fish than any person in Baghdad. I know what a hundred different harbors smell like. I know which stars to follow to find east, and south, and home. I know how to build a raft from driftwood and how to tie a knot that holds in wind and how to stay awake for two days when awake is the only option."
He looked at Hindbad. "That knowledge is in me. The house could burn and I would still have it."
"That," said Hindbad slowly, "is the answer I was hoping for."
The sounds of the house were beginning — doors, voices, the smell of bread. The other guests would arrive soon. The last morning of the story was ending.
"Thank you," said Hindbad. "For the food and the stories and the —" He paused. "For treating me as if my questions were worth answering."
"They were," said Sinbad simply.
~ ~ ~
The Purse of Gold:
After the other guests had come and eaten and the final morning's feast was done, Sinbad stood at the door to see everyone out.
He pressed a purse into each guest's hand as they left — a generous gift, a custom of hospitality that said: you honored my house by eating in it.
When Hindbad reached the door, Sinbad pressed a larger purse into his hands.
Hindbad looked up.
"You deserve more for your patience," said Sinbad, "than a man who came for an afternoon."
Hindbad felt the weight of the purse. It was heavy.
"I can't accept this," he said.
"You can," said Sinbad. "It is not charity. It is payment for being an honest listener. I have told these stories to many people over many years. Most of them listen for the excitement — the whale and the giant and the eagle and the diamonds. You listened for something else."
"What?"
"For what they cost," said Sinbad. "You asked the right questions. Every night you pushed past the adventure and asked: and then what? And what did that do to you? And were you afraid? That is a harder kind of listening and I am grateful for it."
Hindbad held the purse. He looked at the street — the ordinary street, the sunlight on the stones, the other people going about their ordinary days.
"I'm going to think about the river in the mountain," he said. "For a long time."
"Good," said Sinbad. "That is the right one to think about."
"Going into the dark because the beach was worse," said Hindbad.
"Yes."
"It is a good way to think about — a lot of things."
"I hoped you would find it useful," said Sinbad.
They looked at each other for a moment — the old sailor in his fine house and the porter with his load — and something passed between them that was not friendship exactly, because they did not know each other well enough for friendship, but was something in the same family as friendship. Recognition, maybe. The acknowledgment that the other person had seen something real.
"Go well," said Sinbad.
"You too," said Hindbad.
He walked home through the streets of Baghdad with the purse in his belt and the stories in his head. The morning was already hot. His next load was waiting for him at the warehouse by the river.
He picked it up.
It was heavy. It was always heavy.
But he thought about a raft going into a dark cave because there was no better option, and the light that appeared on the other side, and he carried his load down the long hot street toward the river, and the weight of it, somehow, felt a little different.
Not lighter.
But different.
And that was enough.
THE END
~ ~ ~
